rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (sulfuric acid) catalysis. This carbocation is then trapped by acetonitrile, which, after hydrolysis, yields N-tert-butylacetamide. The reactants are simple, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation: carbocation generation, nitrile addition, and hydrolysis to an amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions that often involve aromatic rings, complex functional groups, or specific ring systems. The core transformation is clearly the formation of an amide from a carbocation source and a nitrile, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction. It avoids motifs common to Beckmann rearrangement (oximes), Schmidt reaction (azides), or Friedel-Crafts reactions (arenes).
